prompt: In a student project, internship, or club, which responsibility do you naturally grab first?

options.label: Own the pitch
options.description: I like making the case, winning support, and moving people toward a decision.

options.label: Map the plan
options.description: I want to define the owners, timeline, and next steps so the work actually moves.

options.label: Interview the audience
options.description: I want to understand what users or stakeholders actually need before we decide.

options.label: Work through the numbers
options.description: I’m happiest when I can analyze assumptions, metrics, and financial tradeoffs.

options.label: Figure out the system
options.description: I want to understand how the tools and workflow fit together behind the scenes.

prompt: When a decision is unclear, what feels most natural to you?

options.label: Test a direction
options.description: I’d rather make a smart small move and learn from live feedback than wait for perfect certainty.

options.label: Align stakeholders
options.description: I gather the right people and work toward a shared path forward.

options.label: Break it into drivers
options.description: I want to separate the problem into data, assumptions, and scenarios first.

options.label: Reduce execution risk
options.description: I create a checklist or structure so fewer things can go wrong.

options.label: Clarify system constraints
options.description: I want someone to walk through how the tooling or architecture affects the decision.

prompt: Which compliment feels the most accurate?

options.label: You make things easy to follow
options.description: People say I explain complicated ideas clearly and calmly.

options.label: You keep things moving
options.description: People trust me to coordinate the work and make sure it doesn’t stall.

options.label: You spot the pattern
options.description: I often see the underlying logic before other people do.

options.label: You’re careful with the details
options.description: People trust me when accuracy really matters.

options.label: People open up to you
options.description: Customers or teammates tend to feel heard quickly when they talk to me.

prompt: How do you feel about using new software or technical tools?

options.label: I learn by poking around
options.description: I like exploring a tool directly, troubleshooting, and figuring it out hands-on.

options.label: I like translating tools for others
options.description: I enjoy learning enough to explain the system to less technical teammates.

options.label: I want a clear process first
options.description: I’m fine learning tools when the workflow is documented and predictable.

options.label: I care most about the business case
options.description: I focus more on the decision and outcome than the tooling itself.

options.label: I’d rather stay in the people conversation
options.description: I prefer customer or relationship work over technical exploration.

prompt: A customer or stakeholder sounds frustrated. What do you do first?

options.label: Listen for the real need
options.description: I slow down, ask questions, and make sure I understand the outcome they actually care about.

options.label: Reframe the value
options.description: I help them see the bigger picture and why a path still makes sense.

options.label: Trace the broken workflow
options.description: I want to know whether a process or handoff problem is creating the pain.

options.label: Pull the facts first
options.description: I want the details, metrics, or contract terms before I respond.

options.label: Turn it into a plan
options.description: I define owners and next steps so there is a clear path out of the issue.

prompt: What kind of work feels energizing for hours at a time?

options.label: Negotiating and pitching
options.description: I like persuasive conversations where momentum depends on how I show up.

options.label: Untangling a messy problem
options.description: I enjoy turning a vague issue into something structured and solvable.

options.label: Driving the plan
options.description: I like building structure and then making sure the plan really happens.

options.label: Working through spreadsheets
options.description: Forecasts, analyses, and reconciliations hold my attention well.

options.label: Improving the tool flow
options.description: I like making systems and information move more cleanly.

prompt: When you present to a group, which statement is closest to you?

options.label: I’m comfortable leading the room
options.description: I can speak live, adapt, and stay composed when people push back.

options.label: I’m strongest with a fact-based story
options.description: I present best when I can anchor the story in evidence and logic.

options.label: I prefer facilitating a working session
options.description: I’m good at guiding a group toward alignment more than giving a polished talk.

options.label: I can demo a tool well
options.description: If I understand the workflow, I’m comfortable showing how the system works live.

options.label: I prefer smaller conversations
options.description: I’m better one-on-one or in small groups than in formal presentation mode.

prompt: Which output are you most proud to produce?

options.label: A cleaner process
options.description: I’m proud when I make a workflow more reliable for a team.

options.label: A persuasive narrative
options.description: I like creating the message that wins buy-in and gets people moving.

options.label: An evidence-backed recommendation
options.description: I want my work to show sound reasoning and a clear point of view.

options.label: An accurate model or report
options.description: I’m proud when the work is precise, reliable, and tight on the details.

options.label: A saved relationship
options.description: I’m proud when I help a customer feel heard and stay confident in the path forward.

prompt: In cross-functional work, where do you add the most value?

options.label: Aligning people
options.description: I keep people with different priorities moving in the same direction.

options.label: Sharpening the problem
options.description: I turn a vague goal into a clearer problem statement and decision path.

options.label: Representing the user
options.description: I make sure the team stays grounded in real customer needs.

options.label: Clarifying technical dependencies
options.description: I help make the plan realistic by surfacing what the system can or cannot do.

options.label: Pressure-testing assumptions
options.description: I add value by checking whether the numbers and logic really hold up.

prompt: What kind of pressure is easiest for you to handle?

options.label: Live objections
options.description: I can stay composed in a hard commercial or persuasive conversation.

options.label: A shifting timeline
options.description: I’m good at keeping moving parts organized when deadlines change.

options.label: Incomplete information
options.description: I’m comfortable making progress even when the answer is not obvious yet.

options.label: Technical messiness
options.description: I can troubleshoot and stay calm when a system workflow gets messy.

options.label: High-accuracy work
options.description: I’m steady when mistakes are costly and precision matters.

prompt: If a team keeps repeating the same issue, what is your instinct?

options.label: Create a repeatable process
options.description: I want to fix ownership and make the workflow easier to repeat correctly.

options.label: Find the system issue
options.description: I look for the data or tooling problem underneath the repeated symptom.

options.label: Coach the conversations
options.description: I focus on helping people communicate better and handle the situation differently.

options.label: Check the financial impact
options.description: I want to know how much the issue matters before changing the process.

options.label: Talk to users first
options.description: I want customer or frontline input before deciding what to fix.

prompt: How do you usually approach deadlines?

options.label: Back-plan and track
options.description: I set milestones, monitor progress, and keep follow-through visible.

options.label: Keep the team energized
options.description: I help people stay aligned and motivated so the work lands well.

options.label: Focus on the vital variables
options.description: I prioritize the few factors that matter most to the result.

options.label: Double-check the details
options.description: I would rather be precise than fast if the work has real consequences.

options.label: Anticipate technical blockers
options.description: I think ahead about where tooling or system issues might derail the plan.

prompt: Which internship or entry-level assignment sounds best?

options.label: Shadow customer calls
options.description: I’d enjoy helping move deals or customer conversations forward.

options.label: Help launch a project
options.description: I’d like coordinating workstreams and keeping execution aligned.

options.label: Analyze performance data
options.description: I’d be happy working through budget, pipeline, or KPI data.

options.label: Improve a system workflow
options.description: I’d like documenting requirements and making a tool work better.

options.label: Synthesize user feedback
options.description: I’d enjoy turning user feedback into a recommendation about what to improve next.

prompt: What kind of ambiguity bothers you the least?

options.label: No final answer yet
options.description: I’m okay moving before the full answer is clear if we can test and learn.

options.label: Missing some detail
options.description: I’m fine without every detail if the process and expectations are clear.

options.label: Partial technical context
options.description: I can ask good questions even if I do not know every system detail yet.

options.label: Uncertain customer reaction
options.description: I’m comfortable talking with people even if I do not know how they will respond.

options.label: Imperfect forecasts
options.description: I can still reason well when I have to model scenarios instead of predict exactly.

prompt: Which statement sounds most like you?

options.label: I want to own outcomes
options.description: I’m energized by having a real target or responsibility, not just a task list.

options.label: I keep work dependable
options.description: I like being the person others trust to stay organized and reliable.

options.label: I translate across teams
options.description: I enjoy connecting groups that think differently and helping them work together.

options.label: I solve workflow logic
options.description: I like tracing how tools and process rules create friction or clarity.

options.label: I read what users really need
options.description: I often notice what customers are actually asking for underneath the surface.

prompt: How do you prefer to influence decisions?

options.label: Strong narrative
options.description: I like making a direct recommendation and explaining why it should win.

options.label: User evidence
options.description: I want to ground the decision in user needs and what we have learned.

options.label: Models and scenarios
options.description: I’m most persuasive when I can show the numerical tradeoffs clearly.

options.label: A practical plan
options.description: I like defining owners, next steps, and how the work will actually happen.

options.label: System feasibility
options.description: I add value by clarifying what is technically realistic and why.

prompt: What do you notice first when something underperforms?

options.label: The message isn’t landing
options.description: I think the story, pitch, or framing probably is not connecting.

options.label: The user experience is off
options.description: I focus on whether the customer expectation or experience is mismatched.

options.label: The workflow is weak
options.description: I look for handoff problems, ownership gaps, or process friction.

options.label: The numbers tell a different story
options.description: I want to know which metric or driver is actually causing the issue.

options.label: The technical setup is causing friction
options.description: I suspect the system or tool behavior is creating the performance problem.

prompt: Which kind of follow-up feels most natural?

options.label: Strengthen the relationship
options.description: I naturally check back in with the client or stakeholder to keep trust strong.

options.label: Confirm owners and risks
options.description: I want the task list, risks, and accountability to stay visible.

options.label: Reinforce alignment
options.description: I summarize the decision and keep people committed to the path.

options.label: Document the requirements
options.description: I like making the process, configuration, or requirement explicit and usable.

options.label: Update the model
options.description: I want the forecast or dashboard to reflect the new reality accurately.

prompt: What kind of learning curve feels rewarding?

options.label: How buyers think
options.description: I enjoy learning how to earn trust and move a real buying conversation forward.

options.label: How teams operate
options.description: I like understanding how work really moves so I can improve the process.

options.label: How products work and get prioritized
options.description: I want to understand user needs, tradeoffs, and how improvement decisions get made.

options.label: How finance logic works
options.description: I enjoy learning the structure of statements, metrics, and business performance drivers.

options.label: How a platform is configured
options.description: I like learning the rules, fields, and behind-the-scenes setup of a system.

prompt: Which responsibility would you trust yourself with soonest?

options.label: Represent value externally
options.description: I’d feel comfortable being in the customer or prospect conversation early.

options.label: Run the project tracker
options.description: I’d be ready to coordinate updates, dependencies, and action items across teams.

options.label: Make a recommendation from messy data
options.description: I’d trust myself to analyze the signal and turn it into a recommendation.

options.label: Own a high-accuracy record
options.description: I’d feel good being trusted with a detailed, precision-heavy process.

options.label: Improve a business system
options.description: I’d be ready to document requirements and tighten a system workflow.

prompt: When you hear competing stakeholder opinions, what do you do?

options.label: Look for the customer problem
options.description: I want to know what user need sits underneath the disagreement.

options.label: Push toward a decision framework
options.description: I like giving the group a way to make a practical call despite uncertainty.

options.label: Facilitate alignment
options.description: I guide the conversation so people leave with shared owners and next steps.

options.label: Check the financial soundness
options.description: I want to see which option makes more sense in the numbers.

options.label: Map the technical dependencies
options.description: I focus on what the system constraints imply before anyone over-commits.

prompt: What would make you feel most credible early in a tech company?

options.label: Being trusted by customers
options.description: I’d feel strongest if people remembered me as someone customers trust.

options.label: Reliably shipping the plan
options.description: I’d feel credible if I consistently got complex work over the finish line.

options.label: Finding the best answer in the data
options.description: I’d feel credible by bringing clarity and insight to messy information.

options.label: Understanding how the system works
options.description: I’d feel credible if I could diagnose technical or workflow issues reliably.

options.label: Connecting user needs and priorities
options.description: I’d feel credible if I could tie real user insight to better decisions.

prompt: Which sentence feels most exciting to you?

options.label: Influence revenue directly
options.description: I want a role where my work has a visible connection to growth and momentum.

options.label: Keep complex work on track
options.description: I want to be the person who makes coordinated execution actually happen.

options.label: Shape what gets improved next
options.description: I want a role where user needs and prioritization decisions really matter.

options.label: Be trusted with numbers and controls
options.description: I want work where precision and accountability are central.

options.label: Improve systems people rely on
options.description: I want to make tools and workflows work better for the business.

prompt: Which project outcome would make you proudest?

options.label: The message won support
options.description: I’d feel proud if the team moved faster because I framed the case well.

options.label: Users felt genuinely understood
options.description: I’d be proud if the solution matched what people really needed.

options.label: The project landed cleanly
options.description: I’d feel proud if structure and follow-through made the work land well.

options.label: The numbers were rock-solid
options.description: I’d be proud if the model, report, or control work was highly reliable.

options.label: The workflow became simpler
options.description: I’d feel proud if I understood the system well enough to improve how work moved.
